For each topic, I identified scholarly and policy-based sources outlining the scope of the problem and examined innovative interventions and programs designed to address these challenges. I also developed a capability statement for the Youth and Mental Health Resilience Program in the United States. This involved synthesizing language from previous organizational statements, integrating relevant data on youth mental health trends in the U.S., and articulating the program’s strategic importance. The goal was to produce a cohesive and compelling document that clearly communicated Project HOPE’s capacity and impact in youth mental health programming. A key skill I developed was learning how to draft a capability statement, a professional writing format that was new to me. The main challenge was determining how to structure the document effectively while maintaining high-quality, persuasive language. I addressed this by using previous statements as structural guides and adapting them to fit updated research and program priorities. I aim to begin drafting original written pieces focused on youth mental health and the implementation of Project HOPE’s programs. I am looking forward to transitioning from research synthesis to producing independent written analysis.

I revised and edited the 85-page Youth Mental Health and Resilience curriculum to improve grammatical accuracy, clarity, and overall comprehension. I also continued developing a capacity statement for the Youth Mental Health and Resilience Program in the United States by synthesizing information from reports and prior organizational statements. In addition, I began drafting a research article examining the program’s implementation, analyzing data from initiatives carried out in Nigeria, Costa Rica, and the U.S. Virgin Islands. A key skill I am strengthening is professional writing, particularly in drafting capacity statements and research-oriented documents intended for publication. A challenge I encountered was applying formulas to datasets in Excel while conducting program analysis. To address this, I relied on online tutorials and independent problem-solving to build my technical proficiency. My priorities include continuing data analysis and drafting the introduction and methods sections of the research article.

I continued analyzing data from the Youth Mental Health and Resilience programs implemented in Nigeria, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Costa Rica. My work focused on applying descriptive statistics and basic statistical analysis in Excel to interpret program outcomes and identify patterns across implementation contexts. A key skill I strengthened was advanced Excel proficiency, including the use of formulas, descriptive statistics, and data analysis tools to generate meaningful findings. A challenge I encountered was troubleshooting unfamiliar Excel functions, which often required independent learning. To address this, I utilized online tutorials and external resources to teach myself new techniques, recognizing that building technical fluency requires consistent practice and problem-solving. My priority is to finalize the data analysis and use the statistical findings to begin drafting a research article evaluating the Youth Mental Health and Resilience programs.

I continued analyzing data from the Youth Mental Health and Resilience Program implemented in Nigeria, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Costa Rica. I conducted statistical tests and applied Excel formulas to assess program impact and draw evidence-based conclusions regarding implementation outcomes across different contexts. A key skill I developed was increased proficiency in Excel-based statistical analysis and the ability to translate quantitative findings into meaningful qualitative insights. A significant challenge I encountered was identifying and removing invalid or inconsistent data within the spreadsheet. I addressed this by carefully reviewing entries, validating data points, and systematically cleaning the dataset to ensure accuracy prior to analysis. My priority moving forward is to complete the data analysis so that the findings can be incorporated into a research article evaluating the program’s implementation and impact.

Student was one of the best students the organization had, a very solid and self-motivated intern, a valuable quality in a young employee or intern. She was able to dig into the work with the information provided. The plan was to have her 100% with my team, which is resource development focused, but I noticed that she was interested in gender and gender issues in humanitarian aid. So I set her up with a colleague who works in that field, splitting her time half and half between two teams. This way, she might be able to get what she had been looking for, as well as for us to get the expected deliverables. I think it worked out well. She was able to deliver great work on both teams. Some of the documents and processes we will definitely keep using. Additionally, she is a very pleasant person to work with, never late, and shows up at the office in DC. I work in Atlanta, so we never met in person, but she worked with colleagues in the office. I didn't supervise a lot of students. Maybe one before this time; this is my second. But I worked alongside a lot of them through my 10 years with the organization. I started as an intern myself, so I am definitely familiar with how this works. There were a couple of younger employees in the DC office that I think she could connect and network with. That’s what I was telling her, that she could go to the office one day per week for guidance, but after COVID and work-from-home arrangements, it became so hard for young people to network. Coming out of grad school, when you have this opportunity, that is golden. So I was suggesting working two to three days a week at the office, which I think is a good idea. I don’t really have any suggestions, because the student was so good. If it had been a different person, I might have. Especially at this moment, with foreign assistance and foreign aid funding cuts that we had for the past several months, having an intern who was paid by the university allowed us to have an intern student. We wouldn’t have had an intern on our team over the summer otherwise. So I really loved that opportunity. I think that is great. There are not a lot of schools that are doing that at that level with that many interns. I saw some of the photos of your whole team of interns on LinkedIn the other day. I thought it was great; it seems like a good cohort. Not a lot of other schools are doing that, and some of them still have a half-and-half model, with some interns paid by the organization, which in regular times is fine, but in the current time it makes it really difficult to keep that pipeline of good talent coming through. The main thing is, please keep it up. It’s a great program, and we definitely want to participate again next year. I think the student was very well prepared, so the preparations you are doing on your end seem to be working well. She is very professional, and professionally dressed as well. Coming from grad school or college into the workforce for the first time can be tricky in terms of navigating, especially at a pretty casual place. But she managed that very well. Overall, it was positive. Please keep up the program, because we really enjoyed it. The onboarding process was great. You guys provided interviews with two students initially, and I wish we had space for both of them. I interviewed them both with a couple of my colleagues. The student was great, and the other person that we interviewed was very high quality too. He was just a slightly different fit for the type of work we were looking for. I was thinking that if we could figure out something with one of our program teams, that would have been perfect for him. I still think about how to do this with more than one person, if this becomes an option for next year. People I have worked with are not sure if they want an intern until the intern is around. That is something we are dealing with. The introductory process was great. It was pretty easy. All the forms and everything. That part was not too bad. The student also seemed to have a pretty good understanding of what deliverables she needed to have for the project. I obviously read all the documentation to make sure I understood what she needed. Overall, it was not a bad process to start. Expectations for the students from my side are sort of two levels. The basic tasks: I work in resource development, kind of in between fundraisers and program teams, trying to generate materials to help raise money for various problems around the world, focused on humanitarian and emergency assistance. So there are some day-to-day basic things that we needed help with that we didn't have time for, such as updating databases and helping to edit reports. At the same time, I wanted to make sure that she was not only doing that sort of basic work, but also getting an idea of what working in development actually looks like. That’s why I made the connection with our other sub-team. She was able to work on an actual report while doing some of the data cleaning and work on the report that will actually be published. With us, she also worked on some process improvements and developed a better format for some of our reporting that was taking on a new format. So that was another piece of work that was not basic, but actually a higher-level piece of work. The basic part is that she meets extra help needs, while also trying to introduce the sector to someone who is new to it and start establishing that pipeline of new talent as well, because that's what we look to these programs for. Regarding the application process, from our perspective, both of the candidates were really high quality, so it made it a really difficult choice. I knew that no matter who I chose, it was going to turn out well. I think the preselection process that you just talked about seems like you're curating your potential interns very well, and that makes it a lot easier. The interview is more a verification of the skills needed or of the attitude or culture fit needed. From the small part that I saw of it, I think that seemed to work really well for us. I've been talking to the student about staying in touch and making sure to link in with everybody that she has come in contact with in the organization. I really hope that one day, after she graduates, she finds a role here. I think she would be a great fit. Thank you all for the work that you have done and for keeping the pipeline of young people into this field alive during a difficult time for the sector. I think it's really important work, so I appreciate what you all do. We hope to work together again in the future.

The student was great and awesome to work with. Coming into a business like the organization with no work experience can be intimidating. We are a full-service company that does everything in-house. If you don't have experience with the work that you are interning in prior to working in real estate development and affordable housing, that is something students deal with. She did great and wasn’t intimidated. She started asking questions right away. She wanted to know more. We had her work on several really complicated, difficult projects. I think she got to see firsthand, all the way through my interactions with the leadership committee, the boards, and the executives, how our business works. We have four of us in the department. It’s just me and two others, and the student was working with us. I think she had a great experience. We will do the program anytime in the future. I would say 10 out of 10. I know that there were a couple of other students from the program who were working at the organization other than the student. All I can say as a suggestion is for my department and company as a whole, I don’t know what experience they had. But I think we were a great fit, and everyone from your program was awesome to work with. I don’t have any suggestions. It seems like you were sending great students from your end. Our company seems to be a good fit for people who are looking to get hands-on experience with internships. Actually, I see a lot of day-to-day practical challenges that they might be facing when they are out in the work field. So, no real suggestions from me. I think it was fine. Regarding the onboarding, our team helped with that. We got her set up with the device and email. I know that on their first day, all the interns that were together did a full-day orientation. I wasn’t part of it, so I didn't see if there were any pros or cons there, or with the processes they went through. I would say I was a little overtilted that they were given the same level of orientation as an employee, considering how long they were staying. I think that there were certain things that they could simplify at least. I don’t know if that was on our end or the policy that was put in place. It seems that they were given too serious an introduction. It would have been better if they got to start working in their department sooner and see the work that way. I wasn't there, but I know they did a full day. I didn’t see any issues outside of that. Student matching and specific requirements. What I remember from the student’s resume is that she had done a few other internships before, so she was more ready to step into a work environment. Her professional conduct was great, and everybody loved working with her. I didn't see any turbulence for her as far as knowing how to do well and succeed in a workplace environment. So that was great. I don’t know if GW HIPP has a more appropriate student pool than business students. I think the student’s background was a major in business. I think that’s the right pool of students generally to choose from, at least for the real estate development side of things. For the other departments that we have, I’m sure there is a large mix of different students. I think the way that you guys went about the selection was good. The only real criteria that I’d look for is more well-seasoned students, like ones that have completed most of the program and maybe have some work experience. I think that they will be more ready to step in, because it's a higher level of work. Anything I could delegate to her was fairly complicated. You do want the students to feel comfortable in their own skin so they can unpack the stuff that they haven't seen before. These are skills that they have and can apply to new challenges. I didn’t have any other students as interns, not in my department. But the company did. So we had some from your program and also some from others. I think one was called the Washington Center that we had interns from. There might have been other resources, but this was one that I’m familiar with because they sent resumes to me. I didn’t end up selecting any of them. None of them were a better fit than the student. You guys asked pretty good questions. They made me think about things that I didn’t even consider going into the call, so I think that’s everything I have to say. Both my parents went to GW. I think it’s a great school. I support you guys and would love to continue to work with you going forward. This summer was a success, and I am sure in the future we will be in great shape as long as we keep doing things the way we were and have a lot of opportunities to show students how a company like the organization works. I think it’s a great experience. Students seem to enjoy it. So definitely keep in touch and let me know if I can do anything else to help you.

The student was phenomenal, really phenomenal, in everything that she did over the summer. The experience from start to finish was really great. I know you guys were mostly dealing with Scott Gregory on our end to facilitate the partnership, but from the moment that he told us about the program, when he forwarded The student’s resume, to onboarding, it was really phenomenal. I think the communication was really good. Once she started with us, she was open to meeting a couple of times right before the internship started so that I could talk to her about what her first day would look like. I chatted with her a little bit prior to the internship just to make sure that I was designing it to meet her goals as well and see what her expertise was, and then she really hit the ground running. She worked on quite a few projects with us. I'm a marketing and development strategist, so she jumped right in with some marketing projects with us. She helped out with a lot of different short-form videos for our social media over the summertime, and she was really great. Sometimes we would just chat about an idea briefly or brainstorm, and then the next day, or sometimes later the same day, she would have the first draft or a storyboard or a deliverable ready, with very little instruction needed on our end. I was kind of sitting there being like, oh, let me type up some directions or instructions, and then she would already have a 90% finished project. It's just way above and beyond expectations. On top of some of the work that she did with our social media and content management, she also did an audit of some of our PR materials and provided some recommendations based on her previous experience working with the news outlet. Her biggest project overall was that she worked really closely hand in hand with one of our vendors. So we have a vendor that we're working with on our annual report digital experience, which was just launched, and that same vendor is working with us on an updated video organizational trailer. The student worked really closely with me, really closely with the vendor. I think she really leveled up the creativity of the video. That is with the vendor right now in a 90% completed stage. So we were able to get a lot more done a lot more quickly with her. I think the quality of it was just phenomenal, everything that she brought to the table. That’s my overall impression. I don't think we've been affected as much as some other international organizations. For international projects, we volunteers actually do a lot of the fundraising at a grassroots level for them. We do supply quite a bit of funding from the headquarters level for contingencies, insurance, overhead, project planning, and things like that, but we were actually not large USAID recipients or anything like that. Where we actually got hurt the most was on our domestic side. So for a lot of our domestic projects here in the US through our core program, they were being funded through federal programs like the USDA, Department of Transportation, and the EPA. So we were really hurt on that side of our programming, and just last month, we actually had to do some staff reductions. It was really sad. I think the other intern that we had from the HIPP program, Yoomin, I believe she was reporting to Katie, who was one of the people that got cut. I don't know if you've chatted with Scott about it. She was reporting to a couple of people in her department, so hopefully her internship wasn't too affected. But I think it is really important to still have students who are excited, who are really interested in learning about the issues. I think it's important for us to be transparent with them. I tried to be really transparent with the student about how we got to where we are and where those staff cuts came from, just to give her an understanding of that landscape. I think in terms of us running internships, it's been a long time since EWD has done paid internships, unfortunately. So we've just been doing small, unpaid summer cohorts every year. This was a great addition to have the HIPP program because I felt like I could really give the student substantial, meaningful work and not feel quite as bad about it on my end. You never want to give somebody too much work or anything too far outside of their scope. The outlook is a little weak in terms of our ability to hire interns after their internship right now. We're a pretty small organization, so we have a few people on our staff who have moved from internships into staff positions. It's definitely not the most common. I'm not one of those hiring managers, but in the near future, just with our budget constraints, it may be difficult. Yeah, definitely. Everybody on our team just really loved the student. We were all really sad on Friday when it was her last day. I've heard really great things from the other department about Yoomin as well. I just think the dedication of the interns that you guys sent our way this year was absolutely phenomenal. If in the future we ever have the budget to onboard some of them, that's something we would be interested in. But who knows how long it'll be before we have that kind of budget. I think that onboarding process definitely worked out for us. I would definitely ask Scott Gregory, since I know he was the one who was managing the partnership between EWB and GW. But I know on my end, it was really helpful because I just heard that this program might be happening, and then one day I got a really excellent resume in my inbox, and I was like, yes, let's onboard her as quickly as possible. That's great. We're really small. We have really limited staff capacity. So for me personally, it was nice to just have a resume already in my inbox as opposed to having to spend the time sorting through and then scheduling multiple interviews and really figuring out the perfect fit there. I don't know if any of the other departments have different opinions on it, but I personally was a big fan. No, not any suggestions that are really coming to mind. I feel like the student came in really well prepared for the internship. I heard a lot of great feedback from her throughout the internship that she really enjoyed having her GW cohort meetings on Friday, and then I know at EWB, we had our internal intern cohort meeting every Tuesday, so I know she got a lot of value out of that. I don't know if you guys do it the same for every single intern, with the same start and end date. But I know she was with us 40 hours a week from, I think, June 2 through August 8. It was really good to have a full-time capacity. I think just as great would maybe be somebody who's on 30 hours a week for a couple more weeks. But that might not fit with the way that you guys run things, or that might not be as ideal for the interns themselves. Because I think having somebody who's close to that full-time capacity but has a couple more weeks to really be here would be great. The student was great at jumping right in, but I think it can take people a few weeks to really start and figure out who the people they should be talking to outside of their immediate team are. It was really nice to have that full or nearly full-time capacity. So if there's any way to just stretch it out by another week or two, I think that would be great, but maybe that's just wishful thinking on my end. I didn't think having no engineering background was an issue whatsoever. I also don't have a background in engineering. My background is in international relations, and I kind of stumbled into marketing throughout my career. Honestly, I do think that it was an asset, at least for the marketing perspective, that she didn't have a big background in engineering. I think she's really smart and a really quick learner. So the things that she had to learn, she could. But what she really helped us with actually was humanizing everything to a more digestible level, which is really what we need for marketing, bringing it back down. What is the impact of a project? We don't need to be telling the public about a borehole that we're drilling. We just need to tell them we're providing water to a school where children need water so they can attend every day. I went to American University, so I have a lot of friends who went to GW. I know that GW has a phenomenal communications program, phenomenal media program, and phenomenal international development and international relations programs, and I think those are all really fantastic people to send our way. I thought that remote working mode was great. At the beginning of the student's internship, I asked her what hours she was most comfortable working. She volunteered to work on mountain time hours, so instead of 9:00 to 17:00 East Coast time, she was mostly working 11:00 to 19:00. Sometimes if I saw her send a Slack message before 09:00 my time, I would try to make sure that around 15:00 or 16:00 our time she would log off to maintain a good work-life balance. I thought that it was great. She was really willing to be on any kinds of meetings. She was great at working remotely. We definitely had a few scheduled one-on-ones each week, and then also she was in a lot of other meetings with us. She was always great at participating, participating on Slack. I thought that the time difference was really not too much of an issue, hopefully. It wasn't too disruptive for her life. In the future with other interns, I'd be more than happy to let them work their typical 9:00 to 17:00 East Coast hours. That's okay with me, but she volunteered to do 11:00 to 19:00 instead. Maybe 90% of our staff is based in Colorado at this point, but we have a ton of staff who are in different locations throughout the US. So we're used to having our core hours for communication in the middle of the day, and then everybody's pretty flexible, as long as the work is getting done. Thank you so much for working through this program. I saw your LinkedIn post the other day and recognized a bunch of the other organizations that some of your interns were placed at as well. So I think it's really great that you guys are doing this and just getting the students out there to experience these organizations. It was a phenomenal experience. Like I said a couple of times, I wish that we could keep the student forever. I think everything was really smooth. I don't know if you need a formal assessment or anything. Here on our end, we do an exit interview with an assessment. The student passed with flying colors, but I can send that over to you if that's useful. I don't know if that's a metric that you use or not.

My name is Hameed. I'm the director of research, impact, and evaluation here at Jubilee Housing. I was the one who actually interviewed and recruited the student for this internship a few months ago. My personal experience has been very positive. I believe that she was a really good intern. She learned a lot from us, and we learned from her skills as well. Rachel is the data analyst who was directly working with her hand in hand on some projects that she's going to tell us more about. Overall, I have a very positive impression about her skills and abilities and her willingness to learn and contribute to my team. So that's all I can say. If you have any specific questions that I can answer, I will be happy to do so. But otherwise, I will just hand it over to Rachel for her opinions and feedback that she has specifically. I'm the data analyst, and the project that we had the student spend most of her time on was that we have these two separate databases that aren't always perfectly aligned. And we don't really have the resources to get a platform or an app or someone to spend most of their time making sure they're automatically synced, so it ends up being a lot of manual work. So we had her set up essentially a quick, automated way to line up names, dates of birth, and other identifying information across both of these databases so that going forward, every single week, I can just run her code and then check who is unmatched across these databases and then sync them back up, which is something that I've been meaning to do since I got started at Jubilee Housing a year ago. The student's skill set was slightly different than my own. She had a lot more experience with actually using VS Code and setting up systems more together. I mostly know how to use code to do data analytics. So she was really helpful in teaching me how to use some of these other tools. She was also incredibly resourceful, able to figure out a lot of things that maybe she didn't know in advance by searching the web and asking questions and things like that. I'm hopeful that we were also helpful to her. In school and in other internships, she had worked on data that's somewhat curated, always a little bit. And this was really her first time working with data with all of the challenges that come with data that's not particularly curated. It has all of the mess that comes with daily life and old systems and all of these things, and she said it was really interesting and unique and quite fun and challenging to work with this kind of data, so it was great. Onboarding process, we did not have any issues or challenges around onboarding. I was directly involved in the interview process and onboarding her. But once that stage was finished, the onboarding process within the organization was mainly handled by Rachel, and I don't think there were any issues. But she can provide any additional details on that. Yeah, it went pretty smoothly onboarding her on our side. The only challenge that really arose is one that always is, which is that one of the databases that she needed to learn how to use is Apricot, and it's not very user-friendly most of the time. It's pretty complex, so I found a couple of resources for her. She mostly spent the first week, and I had also created a couple of resources for our internal team on the forms that I set up in Apricot myself that are custom, so she spent the first week just kind of going through them. And then we have a sandbox version of the databases, so I just let her mess around in that sandbox version and get comfortable with it and ask any questions. So the first week, she spent most of her time watching videos and otherwise on a call with me as we went through the system, which I'm sure was not particularly exciting. Of an eight-week internship, a full week to learn the system isn't so bad, but it also feels a little bad that she would spend so much time learning a system that she probably won't touch again for a long time. I thought she was a great match. Internally speaking, she had a lot of experience with the tools that she was using. So for our organization and our purposes, she was great. She was actually able to teach us a lot of stuff. I don't know internally entirely what you're hoping interns will get out of this kind of work. I'm hopeful that she was also able to take stuff with her. Because it is an internship and it's pretty short, but it's still hopefully a pretty great experience that would be good for interns. So if it's super competitive, I don't know, I suppose it would be great if individuals who haven't had previous internship experience and stuff like that also had the opportunity to try. But I know the more competitive things get, the less possible that is. I don't have any suggestions. I don't know if Hameed has any. Honestly, I can't think of anything specifically. But maybe my one suggestion would be to have this kind of conversation before you start, to introduce a new intern in the future. That would be helpful just to get an idea about what to expect and what the process is going to be. Because during the process we had some questions about the schedule, like how long, etc., so I think I would suggest this kind of conversation before we employ any future intern, if you all agree. I think the program is great. We got a lot out of it. Honestly, we are not very resource-intensive, right. We just need to spend time. So it was a boon for us, really.

I'm Yovan Boyd. I serve as the director of the Ward 8 Hub. We're located in Ward 8 in Highlands public housing. We serve the immediate community, and we have people from all over come through our doors. I was looking at our data between January and July, and we served a little over 5,200 people. We have a staff of four here, so it's myself, a community outreach person, a manager of a program called Partnership Independence, and we have a front desk coordinator. We really survive on volunteers because our volume is really high. So it was great to have Jasmine as a part of the team because she is from east of the river. So when she came in from day one, she literally just jumped in. By the time she left, she was able to do every single thing in this office, even covering the front desk when we had guests, so she did navigations. We have a lot of folks that come in that need help. Their utilities may be off, so she would assist them with applying for LAHIV or other programs that are available. She has created flyers for us. She has helped plan special events. We have a lot of community events and activities, so she not only helped plan, but helped facilitate. For example, this community loves bingo, so we use that as an opportunity to educate. So when Juneteenth came, we had a Juneteenth bingo game. It was all educational, so Jasmine actually was one of the ones that facilitated that and did an amazing job. The community really embraced her. So I think, honestly, hands down, she has been the best intern that we've ever had, just because of her comfort level. She's very curious, she doesn't sit around, and she will come and ask, “Is there anything that you need me to do?” So, for example, we survive on donations. We have a lot of people that come in and need clothing, and you all may remember that plane accident when all of those young skaters passed away. One of their families donated all of their clothes to us. So it was probably 300 bags of things that we needed to sort and get ready to go out into the community, and Jasmine just handled everything. She does an incredible job because she's very detail-oriented and she will ask good questions. She's really good at coming up with ideas, especially on how to engage a younger community, which is great because we serve people as young as probably 17 or 18 all the way up until their 90s. Especially, we have a lot of unhoused young people, and I like the way she serves because there's no judgment. Folks that are unhoused, sometimes the lobby doesn't smell that great, sometimes people may have accidents on themselves. You never see on her face like, “Oh my God, I don't want to deal with folks like that.” I think that is very telling on how she'll be as she grows in public health. I know that that's her passion. My background is in public health. I worked in public health for 30 years before I moved into social services. I think with her demeanor that she will really do well. Some of the special projects that she worked on, she helped us plan a career fair for the community by putting all the pieces together, recruiting folks not only to come, but putting together the flyers and the swag bags for them, helping think through what the workshops would look like. She worked with another intern on the history of the hub because the Ward 8 Hub has been here for some while, but it's really grown into something pretty amazing over the last few years, especially with such a small team. When I came here in January of 2022, they just had one workshop. So now we have workshops every day of the week. We provide notary services, we have navigation services, we have career support, we have mental health, and we give away clothes, toiletries, and food. All of these services have morphed into not just serving this immediate Highlands Dwellings community, but people come from a distance to participate in our program as well as to receive services. The ED wanted to capture how it got from where it was to where it is now. Jasmine and one other intern worked on that document. I haven't seen the final product yet, but he just spoke about what an amazing job they did. They did interviews with a lot of key community leaders, and he raved about her. Last week, it was my birthday, so they had a surprise birthday party for me, but they also recognized Jasmine and gave her flowers and balloons and cards. They've never done that for any other interns, so I think that really speaks to the impact that she made and the way that she engaged with the community, because she's just really comfortable. Even when I interviewed her, I was like, “Well, I would love for you to come by the Hub, but I do know that this space is not for everyone, because you encounter some challenging behavior that can take place.” But she was like, “Oh no. That's where I want to be.” I was like, “Okay.” When she came, she did a great job. So last week, as one of our final things, we did some outreach to the unhoused. We went to the shelter at St. Elizabeths, and then we went to a park that a lot of unhoused folks are in. So we were able to provide food, clothing, and toiletries. Jasmine was just all in and never had a shy moment. When we had extra items to distribute, she and I walked the community to engage and hand the rest of the materials and things out. She's an amazing young woman, and I really look forward to seeing her blossom into her career. She even participated in a photo shoot. We have a professional stylist that volunteers with us for clothes. So if someone comes in and they need interview clothes, she'll work with them so that they're comfortable with their interview and how they present themselves. It's called “Style by Jen.” She was doing before and afters. Now she's really just capturing their look after she's worked with them. So she did an intern edition and did a styling with Jasmine. Jasmine was all on board. You don't see a lot of young folks that want to put on a suit, but she put that suit on. She did great pictures. The stylist just gave so many kudos to her, just about how not only she presents herself, but how caring she is towards the community and how open she was to just trying new things and stepping out of her comfort zone. Then I do have one staff person that really has terrible computer skills, and Jasmine worked with him so he can now create flyers in Canva. So she did a wide variety of things when she was here. It was like a countdown to her leaving, and I was like, we're about to lose Jasmine. She was so helpful to us. I honestly don't have any suggestions. We have interns from all over, and honestly yours was so seamless for us. I have nothing. You all did a great job, and I would tell you if I had something.

My name is Matty. I'm the senior supply chain manager for World Central Kitchen. We work out of our main warehouse in Capitol Heights, Maryland. We've got a very large warehouse where we support our teams as they go out to the field with material, ordering, procurement, and everything that's needed for the teams to be able to cook in the field when they deploy into a disaster zone. My experience with the interns was absolutely amazing. The opportunity came up, not out of the blue, but it is something that we had talked about for a couple of weeks, and then all of a sudden Ashley was like, “So Matty, we've got people that are going to support you, and I've got two that are available.” I was like, “Great, let's bring them out.” It was really opportune because my team was growing and my full-time positions were growing. And we had our two interns, that came out. I was really excited about the whole process and the whole event. I actually felt like I was a little bit unprepared going into it, just kind of juggling so much. But they were super flexible, and we were able to really intensely, right at the beginning, identify the things that they were going to work on and then set them on a path and figure out all of those engagement zones. So right at the beginning, they had assisted me in compiling and creating data for one of the things that I'm calling our country pages. Think of it like a one-pager that would allow us, if we've got a hurricane that's impacting Puerto Rico, that's off the map. These country pages will allow us to have one place that I could go and just really quickly get some information that I might need about who to reach out to or what some of the common conditions are that we find. I had them jump into that first. The student jumped into Sudan and just like, I am ready to go into Sudan now. She was able to really capture all the information that I needed and took direction super well. Cali jumped on Mexico at first and started compiling information. From our side, it was going out onto the internet and finding some specific information I needed, as well as scrubbing some of our old WCK information that we have collected from times in the past. The benefit that we got from it is I now have a super solid template to be able to use and replicate over each country. Also, the information packet for as we go into a disaster. Now I know what markers to grab from all of our chats to be able to pull into these country pages. It was a really great start. They had special projects in Guatemala. We were down there for the earthquakes, and I know that they had stepped in to help on the data side with the team down there, kind of data compilation, and worked with our community outreach team to help identify and grab information. Then the biggest project was that we had the feeding opportunity where they planned a project around deploying our food truck into DC's Ward 8 and creating 1,000 meals for the community, where we got everything in place. We showed up at Ward 8 at Bread for the City’s location, cooked 1,000 meals, and provided hot meals to the community that Bread for the City was providing services for. Along with that, we did 800 hot meals live, handed them out individually to community members, and then we provided 200 hot meals to DC Central Kitchen for their evening dinner service. So overall, I was so excited about the opportunity, and I hope that they did well and that they enjoyed their time at our kitchen. They were so good. If they needed direction, they reached out and asked, but it was nose forward and they took it on and ran with it. I was really proud and excited for them. I take fundraising project for granted at times when we talk about possibility. I live in kind of a really good seat where everything's possible, right? And I think that WCK, kind of our whole environment, gears people to learn not to think in those terms. Every time we had these initial meetings talking about the food truck project, I was like, “We've got this. This is what we do all the time.” Every challenge that popped up and kind of got them stuck, don't worry, this is how we'll do it. I need you to reach out to this person. I was really thankful that we were able to get the support from leadership as well as the GW staff to be able to push this project forward because it was a great learning engagement for everybody. Yeah, the suggestion that I would like is kind of an internal and external communications side of it. So internally, we didn't have our communications at the beginning set up right. So we had our HQ staff, the communications department, our safety and security department, development department, and fundraising side of it. Then we had our relief team bubble, which is the team that actually does the activation. I would actually implement something where we had our units within the relief team have capacity to have interns. So think of our community outreach team. They're the first boots on the ground. They're the ones that are engaging with the communities there that have been affected. They're building the initial relationships. They're the ones that are finding and locating community members that need food and that are at risk. We've got our distribution team that works on getting food from the kitchen to those community members. So, super logistics heavy, really working with local support to get food from one place to another. Then our culinary team, who cooks the meals and all of the processes around what it looks like for thousands of meals a day. Then supply chain, which was us. And so for this one, our interns kind of got stuck within that initial process of us learning as well. I didn't realize that 10 weeks was going to go by so fast. Maybe having more markers in between, like, “Hey guys, two weeks over, two weeks is over.” I would create a schedule with interns to be embedded in each one of our units for an amount of time and to be able to get a picture of what each unit looks like and how they operate and see how they can support those units both remotely as well as if we have this project form. Regarding the application process, we could have done better and found better candidates across the board. I think you guys did an amazing job sourcing and getting the right candidates, and I would be hard-pressed to figure out how to improve that.

That was definitely good on her for rolling with the punches like that. We did have her start working on developing a new SharePoint page for our team, and then of course, halfway through that, our team changed. But that was her big project, and then she did a couple of smaller projects for people. I think our biggest suggestion, that a couple of colleagues also noted, and we had another intern from a different institution that aligned with the student perfectly, like start date and end date, and had the same feedback for that team, was that it was a bit short. Eight weeks, we're quite a large organization. It does take a bit of time to get up to speed, even being a full-time intern. And so it was really at week six, maybe, that I was like, oh wow, he's up to speed. She knows who to go to, what she's doing. She's finally got her feet under her, and then it was like two weeks left. I know that obviously you have to accommodate the school year and you want to give people a break and stuff, but that would be the main feedback that I would provide. Onboarding process went well. It was only eight weeks ago, but it also feels so long ago. So much has changed since. For the student, it was kind of tough with the timing. Obviously, Jack, you and I had met for several months before it, and we were like, oh, this just changed, this just changed. The summer was no different. And so we had some ideas in mind before she started, and then right before she started, some things changed. But what I did instead was just set up a lot of calls for her and in-person meetings for her with people on the team so she could kind of get to know what everybody does, their career trajectory, and stuff like that. And I know that was something that she and I talked about at the end, that something she really took away from the internship experience was getting to meet all those people and kind of solidify what she liked and what she didn't like. And then I just onboarded her to SharePoint, how to use SharePoint, where to access it. We have a lot of trainings that I told her, when there's a slow week, please take advantage of the training. There's a lot of tools that you can access by having a Save the Children email. But I think it went well. I think that she was a good match. I think it's hard to say, right? Because I don't know if there was a student out there that would have been a better fit, right? Because we didn't get to see the rest of the resumes. I don't know if there is a different system to use where you send three or four suggestions and do more of an interview style for the organizations themselves. It certainly saved us time not having to do that, especially in a time when things have been so crazy. But again, I can't reflect on that. She was a good fit. But was she the best fit? I don't know because I don't know who else you could select, so I hope that's clear. I don't know. No, I mean, for the most part, we'll try and have some tasks in mind. It's normally not quite administrative tasks, but those longer-term projects that people don't really have the time to dedicate to, and then we have an intern or a fellow that's able to join and kind of push things along. And so most of the time, the expectation is that they can help push things along. But I know, in my experience, we also want to make sure that the internal fellow is getting just as much from us as we are from them. It is an experience where you get to maybe be in a professional environment for the first time or you get to build your network. I did a lot of internships when I was at GW, and most of my takeaways were like, okay, that's fun, I don't want to do that again. And that's a fine enough takeaway. If you figure out something that you don't like, I told the student, if you figure out that I don't like this, that is as good of a lesson as I like this. You're just narrowing down the things that you do and don't want to do. Reach out to me in January when you start looking at it, because we just had a massive team restructure, and we got rid of a lot of our humanitarian team. And so, since it is a humanitarian-facing internship, is there space for an intern or fellow here? Absolutely, yes. There's always something to be done. Will it be humanitarian-focused? I don't know. We do have private fundraising and marketing and comms that focus on humanitarian response. But in terms of our award management programs team, it doesn't really exist anymore, unfortunately. So reach out in January. I will let you know. We also do have a domestic emergencies team, so that's always a possibility. But yeah, reach out in January. Hopefully we'll have found our footing a little bit and I will know the capacity of our ability to meet the program's requirements as well, since it is humanitarian-focused.

Very well. Thank you for your time, and it's a pleasure meeting you all. I'm in the development department of WCK, and it's been a total pleasure to have the student joining us for these weeks. It was during the summer, so it also happened to be during the time when I was taking some time off. She's also been on her own, working with other colleagues, and she's been doing great. So I'm very impressed with her, her skill set, and everything that she brought to the team, and the overall experience has been great, I think, for everybody on the team. Yeah, of course. I mean, the individual giving team. the student has been working on some projects for our team, but working closely with the research team and with the community fundraising team. This was not the communications department. It was still within development, but more in updating the web page and communication-related tasks. She has not had projects with me that I would directly supervise. So I've been the main point of contact, because when we got together in development and we thought, okay, what are these areas and projects where this intern can be of help, we were brainstorming and we came up with different tasks in different teams. So then it was the question of, okay, who can supervise her. It was anyone on the team, so it was me. I was the lucky one. I've been the point of contact, so basically it's been organizing my own team and those direct points of contact of each project and making sure that the student was okay, and that the workload was not too much, but that it wasn't too low, and being that first point of contact for her. If she needed anything, I could help her get that help or, if that was something that I should take over. I'm not sure about preparing them for the internship. I think she came very well equipped, honestly. So, no suggestion there. I feel it's very hopeful that young generations come many times more prepared than we were, especially when it comes to technology and using all of these tools. Maybe the unique circumstances are that this was a first time for WCK hosting interns. So we were the ones not knowing what to do, how to do it, what was expected, what wasn't. So I guess, and this is not feedback for you guys, this is more for us and how you can help us be more prepared to receive them. In our case, in WCK, as you probably know, or Monica maybe you've been handling this, we had Ashley, who's our executive assistant, and she's been the main point of contact with GW. She was getting information, and then she was trying to find time to bring us points of contact together, and that was impossible because we were in different departments. So I guess that my one piece of feedback would be, and I don't know if this is already done because I didn't have access to this first conversation, but to me it would have been helpful to receive something that I could read before. I know, like something ahead of time, but getting something with the purpose and timeline, having that information a little bit before, that would have been helpful. And also, we didn't know there was some sort of assessment at the very end, if there was a final project. They came up with their own project, which was amazing. So leaving it a little loose, I guess, was helpful because they came up with their own creative project to do. But for us, it would have been helpful, I guess, to know from the very beginning. I wish I knew. You said you selected from 200 students. I’m sure they were all wonderful. I think everyone deserves an opportunity, especially when you're 19 or 20, or 20-something. I could not put away any of them, but it is nice to have somebody there where I feel like they're not ready. I mean, we don't expect them to be ready. I think that for people, you know how it goes. It's very busy. We're always very busy, no matter if it's nonprofit, corporation, or whatever. If we have the time to stop and really meet and think deeply about this, I'm sure we would come up with better ideas on how to host these interns and help them have a better experience. Even myself, now when I look back, I'm like, oh, I could have done this with her. I could have done this and that, but it was just not possible because I was dealing with 1,000 things. So bringing, I don't know if this was done again because Ashley was the main point of contact, but giving these ideas on generic things that the interns can do can help, even if it's the most obvious ones, like shadowing a meeting with our client or a donor, or shadowing different personnel to get that experience, that would be something. I didn't do that with the student and now I regret it because I'm like, how did I bring her? Because that was not a project in benefit of the organization. That was something that I could have done for her more, but I didn't because I wasn't thinking about that, and also my director was thinking of tasks, tangible things that need to be done. There's so much they can learn just by having conversations with us or, again, to shadow somebody. See how you do a meeting with any client or anyone. I have one more suggestion. There was Ashley and so many points of contact per intern. Each of the points of contact for each intern, like me, came up with our own little document where we would coordinate the projects and tasks that the intern would participate in or be responsible for. If that could be maybe a template, just as a suggestion. Nothing that the teams would feel like they have to fulfill. But then you were like, okay, if this is helpful, here we go. Something simple, please, simple, but so that the teams can be on the same page. Because I remember when Ashley brought all of them, she mentioned one of my colleagues, Catherine, and she was like, and this one was incredible. I'm going to keep it as a template for other tasks. And I was like, well, I want to see that, right? So maybe looking at whatever Catherine did, so you guys can have a look at it and be like, well, this was apparently very good. Maybe creating a generic one that can be, in case this is helpful, here it goes. You're welcome to create your own one. Oh, what would I expect from an intern? I think that attitude is everything, and I think when you're at that age and in that moment, you need to be open to learn and observe as much as you can. So the student had that perfect attitude where she was serious about the tasks she had to go through, but also she was excited to do more. She even asked, I think she and another intern asked me to be part of some meeting that she had not been invited to, and of course we invited her. So having that attitude is something that we really appreciate, because also, as you know, taking an intern for a few weeks is also additional work for us that, you know, is helpful, but it's usually more added to your plate. So having that nice attitude of, I'm here to help and I'm here to learn, is really wonderful, and if you come with that, you're very welcome, I think. That's me, at least. Everything will be learned, and again, I think no one expects them to come knowing anything. Yeah, so that's what I expect, like having that formal side of whatever you need to do, you need to get it done, but also having that want-to-learn attitude. It must be difficult to do your selection. Many of them are wonderful, and you know, so amazing. We can take more interns, so you guys need to find more places. the student learned a lot about funding.

It went very smoothly, and she was certainly someone we could count on who would take a project and run with it. Her first very large project, the primary project she was working on, was a curriculum for youth to learn how they can create positive change in the world. I had originally thought, okay, she and I will probably work pretty shoulder to shoulder in this. She took that and ran. We would check in, but she did not need a high level of oversight to get us to a really great product. And then that curriculum was used over the last two weekends with the youth. It was very successful. It was something that we used at our Southeast DC location, Ward 8. We had youth very passionate about many things, but particularly ending community violence, and so that was deeply, deeply successful and meaningful to be with the youth in that. This coming weekend, we will be doing a big culminating project that the youth have created. So from that curriculum, what they learned, putting it into practice in the community this weekend. So that was kind of the primary project. The secondary project was a search engine that she created for furniture banks, which is one part of what A Wider Circle does. We provide free furniture to our neighbors that are going without, and we have actually just recently developed a national coalition of furniture banks. So she was creating a search engine for our new website that would allow whether there's someone who is a furniture bank or maybe someone who wants to donate items to a furniture bank, they can identify where furniture banks are throughout the United States. So we're really excited about both those projects being so successful. And yes, she was also helpful on a few smaller projects, things that prepared us for a briefing on Capitol Hill about furniture poverty. So yeah, she was all hands on deck for our many different efforts. Onboarding was immediately far more productive than I had assumed. I mean, we know so many wonderful interns here, and I used to lead our internship program, and so certainly I would say she got settled in and going much quicker than I would have even expected. Again, I think for the first week, we met every day of that week to just make sure, okay, is she feeling settled? Does she know about all of our work and this new project that she's working on? And by the second day, what she'd already had to show me for the project, I was like, oh, okay, she is not going to need a shoulder-to-shoulder partner. She is okay pushing this forward on her own, and that's just with check-ins. So I think that also, I hope, felt very good for her, to feel like this was her creation, truly. Well, I think she talked about networking. I mean, from what we were seeing, I wouldn't have known that that was maybe an insecurity, and we all have that, of just like, okay, I don't even like going to networking events. But no, she did great. I mean, for the first day, we really just took time to go on tours of our location and our work. She was meeting people. She also was able to shadow a totally different department that was very much direct-service kind of work. And I think she really enjoyed that, really connected with our staff there, and our staff also felt that she had done a great job of that. So I actually wouldn't have known that was something that she was having any challenge with, and she seemed to be connecting with other interns as well. I hope, and we made it very clear, that she has us for any type of future, even mentorship, or even if it's just light touches in the future to check in on how she's doing. We're very excited about the kind of work that she wants to do, so needed in our world. The connection is forever, and we keep very long-term mentorship relationships with interns. So I do hope she'll keep up with that as much as she would like. And we also had some great talks. We had a little celebration, just her and I, yesterday for it being her last day, and we really were talking about these next steps that she wants to take and navigating, okay, what could be the next internship that would really provide those skills that she is looking for, and maybe be at the right timing to even make that into a job after school. And I know her passion is very much to join the Peace Corps, and so we are discussing that and potentially any additional education that she'd like to receive. So I have a sense. I mean, she's only going into her junior year, so of course our plans are always like this. We've got one target, and it's always a little bit wavy, but it sounds like she's really, from my perspective, knowing what she needs to do to get into the work she wants to do. I think she's really on track for that. Absolutely. On the advocacy team, which is the team that I lead, we just had the student as our intern. Other departments did have other interns, even from your program and other schools. And so she was able to engage with them and understand the projects that they were doing, but the structure of how we kind of moved through was, again, highly intensive touches for the first week or week and a half, just to be sure that everything's kind of going along, she really has the broad understanding of what A Wider Circle is, and an understanding of what her specific projects are. But after that, we both felt comfortable with one check-in a week, like a very formalized check-in where we go in depth into all of the projects that she was working on. And we're both in the same physical space. A Wider Circle has a few different locations, but she was at this building that I am in, and so on the days when we were here together, we could have more informal check-ins, just to be sure that she's feeling good on that day and has everything she needs. Wow, I would really share them if I had them, but I did feel, and what I kind of understood also from some of the other folks that were supervising students from your program, I think they all came in very prepared, like they took themselves and the work seriously. And I certainly didn't have anything that I could see from like, oh, the student is not really prepared exactly for how this was going to go. No, I think the only thing I might mention is just making sure that young adults in the program maybe know a little bit of just professional standards, even if it's just a reminder of professional standards. Like, okay, if you're having a health issue, try to communicate in advance, that sort of thing. I think the student and I just had, there were a few times where there were a few last-minute things. That's going to happen. And it might have just happened like that, that I had to know last minute. It really was not a big deal for us, but I just gently was like, okay, so in this situation, let's do this so that everyone is kind of aware. But I would say that was maybe something that could be hit on again. Just generally, what are those kind of ways of communicating when you're in a professional space. Oh yes. And thank you all. It is so important to us that internships, this has been such a win-win for us, because to have a paid internship, we are not able to do paid internships. We literally do not have the budget for it, and so to be able to partner, and that is kind of where we see equity, is that internships would be paid. That's something we've been working very hard to get to. We're still not there, but we appreciate a partnership that can allow us to kind of meet that so that everyone has equal opportunities to engage in internships.

I was really fortunate to have had an intern for 35 or so hours a week. She did a great job in a lot of ways. One project she worked on was building a chatbot for the website. She actually kind of co-supervised, with me, the high school intern who was really savvy on some computer coding and AI, and so they worked together. I think she did a nice job on that project. She worked on a couple of database cleanup projects for our CEO. She worked with me on some research, kind of consolidating databases, some really exciting research for us, and she took a first pass at finding conclusions and analyzing mainly numerical data, not qualitative, and turned some of that into slides so we could present it. And then she also spent one day a week doing direct service work, and that was part of a larger team, which I think was good for helping her feel grounded and more connected to our mission. I think she jumped right in with each of the projects, and we didn't start all the projects at once, but with each of the projects there was orientation, and then there was orientation to our organization and some basic things that we went over when she started, maybe for the first couple of hours. So I think she picked it up quickly. The one challenge was we didn't have a great rhythm. Partly it was me being as busy as I am, and partly it was her not having regular check-ins. I would say, with some interns that are working far less, I meet with them once a week. Because she was working a lot more, and sometimes projects were taking shorter than I expected and other times a lot longer than I expected, I tried to get in the habit of saying, please send me an email at the end of the day letting me know what you worked on and what's still on your plate for the next day. At least if we can't get a face-to-face, that would help me budget your time and mine. And she largely did not do that. I tried to address it. I said, you can chat me, or you can text me if it's easier, but especially on work-from-home days, I was like, hello, what are you up to? And she would deliver, especially when I said, I need this in two days from now. But I was also a grad student, and I understood that the things that I think now in my mid-40s take five minutes, take somebody younger and newer to the working world a lot longer. So, like I said, I think it was a combination of both her and me. That kind of interaction, we never totally gelled and worked out. But she was very personable. She was very competent in the projects that she did, and she was communicative if she needed to run late or switch a work-from-home day for some reason. Maybe it brings to mind something that I've done with the clinical interns that I supervise, which is we have a supervision agreement, like we should always call me if X, Y, and Z happens, we should have one hour face-to-face each week, that kind of thing. That could be useful, because I'm going to guess, and this was true for, I know, you hosted two others with us, someone in advocacy and then somebody in another department. One recommendation is, do this year-round. I would love to see that. I mean, I just think it's such an equitable opportunity for people to have paid internship experience and get the support that they do get from GW to kind of round out their experiences. We unfortunately don't have the budget to provide paid internships, at least not yet. It's still a high priority of mine, but being able to host these folks I think was really amazing, and I don't think we could have done half the work that we hoped to this summer were it not for their efforts. So thank you. Adam could speak more to the process of interviewing and such. I didn’t meet the student before the internship. I really think you did a great job helping us select folks, and that was very helpful, bringing folks to us that were the right fits. And from my perspective, which was probably a lot more limited than Adam's, it was very smooth. Well, I think the intentionality of maybe having a mid, maybe like a two- and a six-week check-in, maybe as a triad, the supervisor, the main contact for that student in your program, and then the student themselves, could have been just an intentional way to say, how are things going? I think that's the best idea that I can offer, is maybe to have a three-way check-in. It establishes that there are open lines of communication and, in the worst-case scenario, can help a supervisor know who to reach out to, have a face to go along with that experience. I guess I didn't hear anything from you during the time, so I assume she didn't have any major concerns that you would have brought up. But maybe those conversations could be helpful for all parties. It can help a student to have an ally from the school if they do have a concern with their supervisor, and help lessen that power dynamic. I was so excited when I heard that this program existed and that we were able to take on students. I think it's remarkable. I'm grateful that we could work with the students and this third student whose name I am forgetting because I didn't meet her directly, but I saw her supervisor this morning, and she said, wow, yesterday was her last day. So we really miss her so much. She did such a great job, and she's going to have a bright future in international anti-poverty work. So yeah, all in all, thank you. We're really lucky. Continue to be an advocate for a program like this. If you need a support letter to take to your folks and say, “Look, we need to do more of this,” just let us know how we can help.

I'm Sarah. I'm based in Belfast in Northern Ireland, and great to meet you both. Thank you for scheduling this. I joined Relief International in March, so around five months ago or so. My role in the organization is as a global business development manager, working closely with the director to really generate income and develop new partnerships around corporates, foundations, and trusts globally.
